fixed_beats.title: The Reckoning
fixed_beats.narrative:
You pulled your credit report this morning. The number stared back at you like a verdict. Collections accounts you'd been ignoring. A credit card balance that grew while you weren't looking. The wreckage of a life event you didn't choose, turned into a financial score you have to fix.

But here's the thing — you've built something before. The instincts are still there. The hustle is still there. What's missing is the foundation. Time to build one.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb slides a napkin across the table. 'Three numbers: your credit score, your total debt, your monthly revenue. No judgment — just math. Now here's what I'd sit with: which one, if you moved it first, changes everything else?'

fixed_beats.title: Day One
fixed_beats.narrative:
Your business officially exists as of today. No clients, no revenue, no reputation — just a laptop, an idea, and the savings account that's going to fund this dream until it funds itself.

Everyone you told said some version of 'that's brave.' What they meant was 'that's terrifying.' They're not wrong.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb leans back in his chair. 'Forget the logo, the website, the five-year plan — none of it matters yet. Here's the only question I'd be asking myself this month: who is the one person who'd pay you for what you do, and what's stopping you from asking them today?'

fixed_beats.title: The Wake-Up Call
fixed_beats.narrative:
The tax bill arrived yesterday. It was... larger than expected. Your accountant said, 'We should talk about your entity structure,' which is accountant for 'you're leaving money on the table and I've been telling you for two years.'

Your business made good money last year. Your tax strategy was to make good money and hope for the best. That's not a strategy. And looking at the rest of your setup — no asset protection, no succession plan, key-man dependency through the roof — you realize the business is successful despite your structure, not because of it.

Time to build the scaffolding around what you've already created.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb looks at your P&L and nods slowly. 'The revenue's real, the profit's real — but it's all exposed. One lawsuit, one health scare, one bad quarter from gone. So here's the question I'd ask before anything else: what would you lose tomorrow if everything went wrong today?'

fixed_beats.title: Six-Month Check-In
fixed_beats.narrative:
Half a year in. The spreadsheet tells one story — the numbers, the trajectory, the cold math of progress. But sitting here at the end of month six, the real story is about what you've learned about yourself as much as what you've built.

Are you the kind of person who fixes the foundation or skips to the roof? Who invests in protection or rolls the dice? Who builds systems or stays the hero? Six months of answers are on the board.

fixed_beats.title: Tax Season
fixed_beats.narrative:
A year in, and the tax bill is coming due — the first real test of whether you kept a reserve or spent every dollar as it came. Money earned, spent, borrowed, repaid; you're not the same person who started this.

This is still Part 1 — you're building the machine. Keep going. The payoff, when the whole thing comes online, is still ahead of you.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb glances at your books before tax season hits. 'A pro sets the tax money aside before they ever see it. Here's what I'd ask: if the bill landed tomorrow, could you cover it without touching the business?'

fixed_beats.title: Part 1 — The Machine Comes Online
fixed_beats.narrative:
Month eighteen. This is what Part 1 has been building toward. Everything you've assembled — the leverage, the credit, the protection, the policy — is about to click into one machine that runs whether you work or not.

Get it right this month and Part 2 becomes a different game entirely: not grinding for revenue, but watching the engine compound into freedom.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb almost smiles. 'This is the part the people who quit early never get to see. Finish assembling the machine — then in Part 2 your job stops being to work harder and starts being to let it run.'

fixed_beats.title: Part 2 — Letting It Run
fixed_beats.narrative:
Two years in, deep into Part 2. The machine is running now — passive income, protection, systems, people. Your job isn't to build more; it's to let it compound, keep more of it from the taxman, and actually live the life all this was for.

Twelve months to the finish — and your final Paradise score.

fixed_beats.title: The Test
fixed_beats.narrative:
Everything you've built gets tested this month. Not by you — by circumstances. The market shifts. A key relationship is strained. A number you've been watching finally crosses a line.

This is the moment the foundation either holds or cracks. Every decision you made — every system you built, every protection you put in place, every relationship you maintained or neglected — shows its weight right now.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb calls instead of meeting in person. His voice is calm. 'This is the month that separates the builders from the survivors. Everything until now was preparation. Here's what I'd be asking myself: did you build it to hold?'

fixed_beats.title: The Verdict
fixed_beats.narrative:
Thirty-six months. The journey ends — or rather, this chapter of it does. The numbers will tell one version of the story. The radar chart will show six dimensions of what you built.

But the real verdict is simpler: Did you build something that lasts? Did you build something that works without you? Did you build a life worth living while you built the business?

The answers are in the choices you made — all of them, from month one to right now.
fixed_beats.mentor_line: Marcus Webb shakes your hand. 'I've seen a lot of people sit where you're sitting — most are still stuck where they started. You're not. Here's the question that matters more than the numbers: did you build a life worth living while you built this?'

monthly_cliffhangers.text: You're doing real business now — but the business is still legally just you. No LLC means every client and every contract is a direct line to your personal savings. One bad dispute could take it all.
A client offhandedly mentioned 'getting their lawyer involved.' Probably nothing. But you have no LLC — no legal shield. If they're serious, there's nothing between them and everything you own.
You felt run-down all week. It hit you: you ARE the business. If your body quit tomorrow, the income would stop cold — and you have nothing in place to replace it.
You've built something real now — accounts, maybe property, serious income. And it's all sitting in the open. No trust, no asset protection. One determined lawsuit could reach all of it.
Your best employee asked for 'a few minutes to chat' tomorrow. Those words never precede good news.
You overheard two team members talking about job postings. They went quiet when you walked by.
The banker asked you to come in next week. She didn't say why. She sounded... careful.
Your biggest client's contract renews next month. They haven't said anything. The silence is louder than a complaint.
A competitor just launched something that looks a lot like your offer. Coincidence? Doesn't matter — the market noticed.
The quarterly tax estimate arrived. The number has an extra digit this time.
The phone rang as you were locking up. You didn't answer. The voicemail light blinks — it can wait until morning. Or can it?
An email arrived at 11:47 PM. Subject line: 'We need to talk.' You'll open it tomorrow.
The market report came out today. The trends are shifting. Your industry is in the crosshairs — for better or worse.
You realized today that you can't remember the last time you did something just for fun. Not networking, not 'productive rest.' Just fun.
